questions: For the ~20 types defined directly in useTimelineData.types.ts (TimelineDataRef, TimelinePendingOpsRef, TimelineApplyEdit, etc.), should they move into useTimelineState.types.ts (which already defines the context interfaces that consume these types) or into a new lib/timeline-state-types.ts file? My default is useTimelineState.types.ts since it's already the natural consumer.

For useExternalDrop extraction: some of the 'pure' functions like createTrack/createTrackAtTop mutate the dataRef in place. Should these still be extracted (they're pure in the React sense — no hooks — but not functionally pure), or only extract the truly stateless transforms?

success_criteria.criterion: All 26 test files / 94 tests pass after every step
useTimelineData.types.ts is deleted with zero remaining imports
useExternalDrop.ts hook body is under ~200 lines (pure functions extracted)
useClipEditing.ts hook body is under ~500 lines (pure functions extracted)
No new files beyond external-drop-utils.ts and clip-editing-utils.ts
All changes are type-level or code-organization only — no behavioral changes

assumptions: The 3 console.logs in TimelineEditor.tsx are debug leftovers that should be removed, not intentional instrumentation (the brief says 'debug leftovers' and they have no feature-flag gating).

Moving types to useTimelineState.types.ts is preferred over creating a new file, since it already imports most of these types and CLAUDE.md says 'every change should make the codebase smaller or more explicit'.

For useExternalDrop, all standalone functions before line 683 (the hook definition) are extraction candidates, including the ref-mutating ones like createTrack — they don't use React hooks and are testable in isolation.
The deferred items (TimelineCanvas extraction, contexts.ts params, ClipAction.tsx) are intentionally out of scope for this round per the brief's 'focus on highest-impact items' constraint.
useTimelineState.ts line 29 re-exports types from useTimelineData.types — this re-export chain will need updating to point to the new source.
